title: Auditable Agent Delegation Chains for Enterprise SaaS
description: As enterprises deploy agent swarms, the question 'who authorized this agent to act?' becomes a compliance requirement. Bolyra's Delegation circuit provides cryptographic proof of the full authorization chain (human → operator → agent → sub-agent) with one-way scope narrowing, satisfying auditors without revealing internal org structure.

rationale.demand: Web search evidence returned zero results and no URLs were cited; the need for auditable agent delegation chains is logically sound but entirely unvalidated by external sources.
rationale.timing: No competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or regulatory deadlines were provided; the timing argument rests on general 'agent swarms are coming' intuition with no concrete anchors.
rationale.fit: This is Bolyra's sweet spot — Delegation circuit, HumanUniqueness, AgentPolicy, and IdentityRegistry all map directly with only minor extensions needed, and ZKP provides a genuine privacy advantage over conventional audit logs.
rationale.feasibility: The MVP builds on existing circuits and SDK scaffolding with 13 days of work, but delegate() promotion from stub to full implementation is non-trivial and the spec says it's a v0.3 stub, meaning real engineering risk remains.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core primitive — already proves human→agent→sub-agent chains with max 3 hops, one-way scope narrowing (delegateeScope ⊂ delegatorScope), expiry narrowing, and EdDSA delegation token signatures. Directly satisfies the 'who authorized this agent?' audit requirement without revealing the delegator's identity or internal org hierarchy.

Anchors the top of every delegation chain to a unique human. The nullifier (Poseidon2(scope, secret)) proves the chain originates from a real person within an enterprise scope without revealing which person — critical for auditors who need 'a human approved this' without PII exposure.

Each agent in the chain carries a verifiable credential with cumulative-bit permissions and expiry. Auditors can verify that every agent in the swarm operated within its authorized permission set and that no credential was expired at action time.

On-chain delegation verification with chain-state tracking, delegation nullifier replay protection, and hop count enforcement (max 3). Provides the tamper-proof audit log — every delegation event is verifiable on Base without trusting the enterprise's internal logs.

SDK already exposes createHumanIdentity, createAgentCredential, and the delegation stub. Enterprise SaaS integration requires promoting delegate() from stub to full implementation and adding chain-serialization helpers for audit export.

new_components_needed.description: Promote delegate() from v0.3 stub to full implementation: chain construction, proof generation for multi-hop delegation, and chain verification. Add serializeDelegationChain() and verifyDelegationChain() for audit export/import.

Delegation chain audit format specification — a JSON-LD or CBOR envelope that packages the sequence of delegation proofs with timestamps, scope masks, and on-chain verification references into an auditor-consumable artifact. Extends draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01.

Enterprise SaaS connector SDK (REST middleware / OpenAPI spec) that intercepts agent actions, attaches delegation chain proofs, and exposes a /audit/delegation-chain/{txHash} endpoint for compliance teams. Target: Express/FastAPI middleware.

DelegationAuditLog contract or extension to IdentityRegistry that emits structured events per delegation verification (chainId, hopCount, scopeMask, timestamp) for off-chain indexing by enterprise SIEM/GRC tools. Current contract verifies but doesn't emit rich audit events.

GRC platform adapters — connectors for Vanta, Drata, or ServiceNow GRC that pull delegation chain audit logs and map them to SOC 2 / ISO 27001 control evidence. Initially a webhook + JSON transform layer.

positioning_alignment: This is the canonical privacy-layer-under-incumbents play. Okta, Auth0, and Azure AD manage machine identity and authorization but cannot cryptographically prove delegation chains without exposing org structure. Bolyra slots underneath as the ZKP verification layer: the enterprise keeps using Okta for identity management, but delegation proofs are generated and verified via Bolyra's circuits. Auditors get mathematical certainty ('this agent was authorized by a human within scope X, through at most 3 hops, all unexpired') without seeing the human's identity, the org chart, or internal permission policies. This is unachievable with OAuth scopes or SPIFFE alone, creating a moat that incumbents would need 12–18 months of ZKP R&D to replicate.

deliverables.name: delegate() full implementation
deliverables.description: Promote SDK delegate() from stub to working function: construct delegation chain objects, generate Groth16/PLONK proofs for multi-hop delegation (up to 3 hops), verify chain integrity. Wire existing Delegation.circom witness generation through snarkjs.

deliverables.name: serializeDelegationChain()
deliverables.description: Serialize a sequence of delegation proofs (human→operator→agent→sub-agent) into a portable JSON envelope with scope masks, expiry timestamps, and on-chain tx references. Deserialize and re-verify on import.

deliverables.name: verifyDelegationChain()
deliverables.description: Standalone verifier that takes a serialized chain envelope, checks each hop's ZK proof, validates cumulative-bit scope narrowing across hops, and confirms expiry ordering. No on-chain call needed—pure cryptographic audit.

deliverables.name: Delegation chain audit format spec
deliverables.description: Short specification (extending draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01) defining the JSON envelope schema for delegation chain audit artifacts: proof array, scope mask per hop, timestamp bounds, nullifier commitments, and optional on-chain anchors. Purely technical, no commercial framing.

deliverables.name: CLI demo: audit-chain
deliverables.description: CLI script that creates a 3-hop delegation chain (human→operator→agent→sub-agent), serializes it, then verifies it—demonstrating the full audit loop. Runs entirely locally with mock identities.

deliverables.name: Integration tests for multi-hop delegation
deliverables.description: Mocha test suite covering: 1-hop, 2-hop, 3-hop chains; scope narrowing enforcement; expiry narrowing; 4th-hop rejection; tampered-proof detection; serialization round-trip. Both fast (witness-only) and slow (full proof) modes.

risks: delegate() promotion is the largest single task—Delegation circuit witness generation is working but SDK wiring touches proof serialization, key loading, and error handling across JS and Python bridges
Multi-hop chain verification at 3 hops means 3 sequential proof generations in the slow path (~6min total)—may need to document that production use requires rapidsnark
Audit format spec could scope-creep into enterprise requirements (SIEM integration, log forwarding) before any real customer validates demand
Web search evidence returned nothing—demand signal is speculative, based on Okta machine-identity trend extrapolation rather than observed buyer behavior
Python SDK bridge for delegation chains adds complexity since all proving is JS-only—may need to defer Python support to post-MVP
mvp.success_criteria: A developer can npm install @bolyra/sdk, run the CLI demo, and produce a cryptographically verifiable 3-hop delegation chain audit artifact in under 60 seconds (fast mode). The test suite passes in both fast and slow modes. The spec is self-contained enough that a second implementer could build a compatible verifier from the spec alone.

title: DID-Native ZKP Credentials for Zero-Trust Agent Frameworks
description: The arxiv paper (2505.19301) validates the zero-trust + DID + VC direction for agentic AI but uses conventional VCs without ZKP selective disclosure. Bolyra's did:bolyra method and proof envelope format are purpose-built for this gap — agents prove specific claims (e.g., 'authorized for financial ops under $10K') without revealing the full credential.

rationale.demand: Web search evidence returned zero results and the opportunity cites a single arxiv paper with no named buyers, funded competitors, or procurement signals — purely anecdotal.
rationale.timing: No competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or regulatory deadlines were verified; the arxiv paper alone does not anchor a market window.
rationale.fit: AgentPolicy selective disclosure, Delegation scope-narrowing, did:bolyra nullifier resolution, and the IETF proof envelope draft map directly with minimal new work required — this is what Bolyra was built for.
rationale.feasibility: MVP deliverables reuse existing SDK and circuit primitives with only spec/demo work, but the 6-day estimate assumes the VC-to-proof-envelope bridge is straightforward when JSON-LD context mapping may surface edge cases.

mapped_primitives.usage: Already proves selective disclosure of permission bits — an agent can prove 'FINANCIAL_SMALL is set' (bit 2) without revealing the full 8-bit bitmask. This is exactly the 'authorized for financial ops under $10K without revealing full credential' pattern the arxiv paper lacks.

The DID method is purpose-built for ZKP-native resolution. Each did:bolyra identifier resolves to a verification method that accepts Groth16/PLONK proofs rather than conventional VC signatures. Zero-trust agent frameworks need a DID method that doesn't leak identity on resolution — did:bolyra's nullifier-based approach prevents correlation across scopes.

The IETF draft defines the proof envelope content type and mutual authentication flow. Zero-trust agent frameworks need a wire format for exchanging ZKP-backed credentials — this draft is that format. The arxiv paper's framework would slot Bolyra proofs into its trust-negotiation phase via this spec.

Zero-trust agent frameworks involve chains of delegation (user → orchestrator agent → sub-agent). The Delegation circuit enforces scope narrowing and expiry narrowing on-chain with max 3 hops, which maps directly to the paper's trust chain requirements.

The human-in-the-loop root of trust. Zero-trust frameworks need to anchor agent authority to a verified human principal. HumanUniqueness provides sybil-resistant proof that a unique human authorized the delegation chain, without revealing who.

On-chain verification of handshakes and delegation chains. Zero-trust frameworks need a public verification anchor — the registry's atomic handshake verification (Groth16 + PLONK in single tx) and delegation nullifier replay protection serve this role.

The SDK's createAgentCredential + proveHandshake + verifyHandshake functions provide the developer-facing API that zero-trust frameworks would integrate. The error classes (ScopeEscalationError, ExpiredCredentialError) already model the failure modes the paper identifies.

new_components_needed.description: VC-to-proof-envelope bridge spec: a mapping document showing how W3C Verifiable Credentials (as used in the arxiv paper's framework) can be wrapped in Bolyra proof envelopes. Defines how VC claim predicates map to AgentPolicy permission bits and how a VC issuer can mint a Bolyra-compatible credential commitment.

Zero-trust agent framework adapter (e.g., for frameworks like LangGraph, CrewAI, or AutoGen) that intercepts agent-to-agent calls and injects Bolyra mutual ZKP authentication at the transport layer. Builds on existing langchain-bolyra-tool and crewai-bolyra-tool stubs.

VC credential import function: createAgentCredentialFromVC(vcJwt, operatorKey) that extracts claims from a standard W3C VC JWT, maps them to Bolyra permission bits, and produces an AgentCredential. This bridges the conventional VC world to ZKP selective disclosure.

Conformance test vectors for the VC bridge — extend the existing 37-vector suite with cases covering VC-imported credentials, selective disclosure of VC-originated claims, and interop with DID methods referenced in the arxiv paper (did:web, did:key).

positioning_alignment: This opportunity is a textbook privacy-layer-under-incumbents play. The arxiv paper validates that the industry is converging on DID + VC + zero-trust for agents, but using conventional crypto. Bolyra slots underneath as the ZKP layer that upgrades 'verify credential' to 'verify claim without seeing credential.' Incumbents (Microsoft, Google, framework vendors) build the orchestration and trust frameworks; Bolyra provides the cryptographic primitive they call into. The VC bridge adapter makes this frictionless — teams already using W3C VCs import them into Bolyra and get selective disclosure for free, without rearchitecting their identity stack.

deliverables.name: VC-to-Proof-Envelope Bridge Spec
deliverables.description: Mapping document showing how W3C Verifiable Credentials translate to Bolyra proof envelopes. Defines the JSON-LD context extension, credential-subject-to-circuit-input mapping for AgentPolicy bits, and a worked example showing a VC with 'authorized for financial ops < $10K' converted to a Groth16 selective disclosure proof.

deliverables.name: Zero-Trust Agent Auth Example
deliverables.description: Standalone Node script (~200 LOC) demonstrating the full flow: human enrolls via HumanUniqueness, delegates to orchestrator agent via Delegation circuit (scope-narrowed to FINANCIAL_SMALL), orchestrator sub-delegates to worker agent (further narrowed to READ_DATA only), worker proves its authorization to a verifier without revealing the chain. Uses existing SDK functions end-to-end.

deliverables.name: did:bolyra Zero-Trust Resolution Profile
deliverables.description: Short addendum to did-method-bolyra.md defining a 'zeroTrustAgent' verification relationship type. Specifies that resolving a did:bolyra agent DID returns only a proof-acceptance descriptor (supported curves, max delegation depth, accepted permission bits) — no identity material leaks on resolution. ~2 pages.

deliverables.name: Conformance Test Vectors for Agent Selective Disclosure
deliverables.description: 10-15 test vectors in the existing conformance runner format: valid/invalid AgentPolicy proofs with specific permission bit patterns, delegation chains at depth 1-3, expired credentials, scope escalation attempts. Proves the arxiv paper's threat scenarios are handled.

deliverables.name: Technical Blog Post: ZKP Selective Disclosure vs Conventional VCs for Agent Auth
deliverables.description: Educational comparison referencing arxiv 2505.19301. Shows what conventional VCs leak (full credential on every presentation) vs what Bolyra's approach reveals (only the proven claim). Includes the demo's output as a worked example. Pure technical content, no CTA.

risks: Arxiv paper is academic — zero-trust agent frameworks may not adopt W3C VCs in practice for 18+ months, making the bridge spec premature
No web search evidence found for real-world demand — the 17/25 demand score may be optimistic
Solo-founder time: 9 days competes with protocol-autoresearch and differentiation-autoresearch loops already running
Bridge spec risks over-specifying a VC format that W3C Agent CG hasn't standardized yet — may need rework
mvp.success_criteria: Demo script runs end-to-end with existing npm test infrastructure (no new dependencies). Bridge spec gets at least 2 external reviews from W3C CCG or DIF members within 60 days of publication. Conformance test vectors pass in both fast (witness-only) and slow (full proof) modes. Blog post is technically accurate enough to cite in a future IETF draft.

title: LangChain Agent Authentication Plugin
description: LangChain lacks native agent-to-agent authentication. Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit could provide a LangChain middleware that lets agents prove credentials (model hash, operator, permissions) without revealing private keys. The existing langchain integration in integrations/langchain/ is a natural launchpad.

rationale.demand: Web search evidence returned zero results and no URLs were cited; demand is purely inferred from the general popularity of LangChain, not from specific buyers, GitHub issues, or funding data requesting agent-to-agent ZKP auth.
rationale.timing: No competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or regulatory deadlines were cited; the timing assessment itself says 18+ months, and there is no evidence of a closing window.
rationale.fit: AgentPolicy, Delegation, and the existing langchain integration stub map directly with minimal new circuit work — the main effort is promoting the stub to a real integration, which is SDK/integration work not cryptographic novelty.
rationale.feasibility: The MVP spec is well-scoped at ~7 days using the existing Python subprocess bridge and published SDK primitives, though the lack of validated demand makes build priority questionable.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core proving engine — LangChain agents use AgentPolicy to generate PLONK proofs of their credentials (model hash, operator key, permission bitmask, expiry) without revealing private keys. The selective disclosure capability lets agents prove only the required permission bits for a given tool invocation.

Enables multi-agent LangChain chains where a parent agent delegates narrowed permissions to sub-agents (e.g., a planning agent delegates FINANCIAL_SMALL to an execution agent). The 3-hop limit maps naturally to typical LangChain agent hierarchies (orchestrator → planner → executor).

On-chain root of trust for agent credential commitments. LangChain middleware queries the agentTree Merkle root to verify proof validity. Root history buffer (30 entries) provides a grace window so proofs generated a few blocks ago still verify.

The middleware wraps createAgentCredential and the AgentPolicy proving path. verifyHandshake provides the verification side. permissionsToBitmask maps LangChain tool permission requirements to Bolyra's 8-bit encoding.

Existing stub in integrations/langchain/ — this IS the integration point. Needs to be promoted from stub to a real BaseTool that wraps credential proof generation and a LangChain callback handler that intercepts tool calls to enforce permission checks.

new_components_needed.description: LangChain middleware/callback handler that intercepts agent tool invocations, extracts required permission scope from tool metadata, and calls AgentPolicy proof generation + verification. Needs to handle both sync and async LangChain execution paths.

LangChain credential provider — a BaseCallbackHandler or RunnableConfig extension that attaches a Bolyra AgentCredential to the agent's runtime context so proofs can be generated on each tool call without re-initializing.

Permission mapping layer: a declarative schema that maps LangChain tool names to required Bolyra permission bitmasks (e.g., SerpAPI → READ_DATA, Stripe tool → FINANCIAL_SMALL). Ships as a JSON config with SDK helper to compile it.

LangChain integration spec documenting the authentication flow: credential attachment → tool call interception → proof generation → verification → allow/deny. Needs to cover error semantics (ExpiredCredentialError, ScopeEscalationError) mapped to LangChain's error handling.

Example chain demonstrating end-to-end: a LangChain ReAct agent with Bolyra credentials proving FINANCIAL_SMALL permission before executing a payment tool, with a second agent in the chain receiving delegated READ_DATA scope.

positioning_alignment: Directly executes the privacy-layer-under-incumbents strategy. LangChain is the dominant agent framework (~80k GitHub stars, massive ecosystem) but has zero native authentication — agents call tools with ambient authority. Bolyra slots in as invisible infrastructure: developers pip install the plugin, annotate tools with permission requirements, and get ZKP-backed auth without understanding cryptography. This creates a distribution wedge — every LangChain developer who adopts the plugin becomes a Bolyra user, and their deployed agents become on-chain credential holders in the agentTree. The selective disclosure property is the key differentiator vs. OAuth/API-key approaches: an agent proves it HAS financial permissions without revealing its operator identity or full permission set, which matters for competitive multi-agent marketplaces where operators don't want to expose their capability surface.

deliverables.name: bolyra-langchain callback handler
deliverables.description: Python BaseCallbackHandler that intercepts on_tool_start, looks up required permission bitmask from a tool→permission map, calls AgentPolicy proof generation via the Python SDK subprocess bridge, and attaches the proof to the tool invocation metadata. Handles both sync and async paths via LangChain's AsyncCallbackHandler.

deliverables.name: BolyraCredentialProvider (RunnableConfig extension)
deliverables.description: A lightweight wrapper that loads an AgentCredential (operator key, model hash, permission bitmask, expiry) into LangChain's RunnableConfig so the callback handler can generate proofs per-tool-call without re-initializing. Stores credential in config['configurable']['bolyra_credential'].

deliverables.name: Tool permission map + helper
deliverables.description: JSON schema mapping LangChain tool names to required Bolyra permission bitmasks (e.g. SerpAPIWrapper→READ_DATA, StripeChargeTool→FINANCIAL_SMALL). Ships as a default JSON file plus a Python helper that merges user overrides and validates cumulative-bit encoding rules.

deliverables.name: Verifier middleware
deliverables.description: A LangChain tool wrapper (or second callback handler) on the receiving/server side that verifies incoming AgentPolicy proofs before allowing tool execution. Calls verifyHandshake via the Python subprocess bridge. Rejects expired credentials and insufficient permissions.

deliverables.name: Multi-agent delegation example
deliverables.description: A working LangChain example: orchestrator agent delegates FINANCIAL_SMALL to an executor sub-agent using the Delegation circuit. Demonstrates 2-hop chain (orchestrator→executor) with scope narrowing. Uses LangChain's AgentExecutor or LCEL chain.

deliverables.name: Integration docs + quickstart
deliverables.description: README with pip install instructions, 10-line quickstart (attach credential, add callback, run agent), architecture diagram showing proof flow, and API reference for the permission map schema. Published as integrations/langchain/README.md.

risks: LangChain's callback/RunnableConfig API is unstable — breaking changes in langchain 0.3+ could require rework within weeks of shipping
Python SDK subprocess bridge adds ~500ms latency per proof generation — may be unacceptable for tool-call-heavy agent chains; need to benchmark and document the perf envelope

Adoption risk: LangChain developers may not yet perceive agent-auth as a problem worth solving — the plugin may ship to crickets without organic pull from a real security incident or framework endorsement
Permission map maintenance: the default tool→bitmask mapping is inherently incomplete — community tools won't be covered, pushing complexity onto the user
mvp.success_criteria: A developer can pip install bolyra-langchain, add 3 lines to an existing LangChain AgentExecutor (credential + callback), and have every tool call automatically generate and verify an AgentPolicy ZKP — proven by a passing end-to-end test that shows a tool call rejected when the agent lacks the required permission bit.

title: Machine-Native Identity Layer for Agentic Wallets
description: The 2026 shift to agentic wallets (Privy, Coinbase, TON) creates demand for self-sovereign agent identity that isn't just a keypair. Bolyra's AgentPolicy proof bundles model hash, operator signature, and permission scope into a single verifiable credential — giving wallets reputation-building primitives without doxxing the operator.

rationale.demand: Web search evidence returned zero results and the opportunity cites no verifiable URLs, named buyers, or engagement metrics — demand is entirely asserted without proof.
rationale.timing: No competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or regulatory deadlines are cited; 'agentic wallets' from Privy/Coinbase/TON are named but without shipped product dates or adoption numbers.
rationale.fit: All three core circuits (AgentPolicy, HumanUniqueness, Delegation) plus IdentityRegistry and the SDK map directly with only minor modifications needed — this is a genuine sweet spot for existing Bolyra primitives.
rationale.feasibility: MVP spec is well-scoped with concrete deliverables reusing existing SDK and circuits, though the Privy adapter depends on an external signer interface that may shift, and the 7-day estimate assumes stable upstream APIs.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core primitive — an agentic wallet presents an AgentPolicy PLONK proof to prove it holds a valid credential (model hash, operator EdDSA signature, permission bitmask, expiry) without revealing the operator's identity or full permission set. Selective disclosure via requiredScopeMask lets wallet counterparties verify 'this agent can do FINANCIAL_SMALL' without learning the agent also has SIGN_ON_BEHALF.

Operator-behind-the-wallet attestation — when a wallet needs to prove a unique human operator authorized it (e.g. for KYC-light onboarding or sybil-resistant airdrops), the operator generates a HumanUniqueness proof scoped to the wallet provider. The nullifier prevents one human from registering multiple wallet agents in the same scope.

Sub-agent wallet hierarchies — an operator delegates a narrowed credential to a trading agent, which sub-delegates further to a gas-optimization bot. The delegation circuit enforces scope narrowing (trading bot gets FINANCIAL_SMALL but not SIGN_ON_BEHALF) and expiry narrowing, giving wallets composable permission chains without on-chain identity linkage.

On-chain root of trust — wallet providers or DeFi protocols call verifyHandshake to atomically verify both the human-operator proof (Groth16) and the agent-wallet proof (PLONK) in a single transaction. Root history buffers allow async verification when the wallet's Merkle root is a few blocks stale. Delegation nullifier replay protection prevents credential reuse after revocation.

Client-side integration — wallet SDKs (Privy, Coinbase) call createAgentCredential to mint a wallet identity, proveHandshake for mutual auth with counterparty agents, and permissionsToBitmask to map human-readable permissions to the circuit's 8-bit encoding. The error classes (ExpiredCredentialError, ScopeEscalationError) provide wallet-appropriate failure semantics.

Portable wallet identity — each agentic wallet gets a did:bolyra identifier resolvable to its AgentPolicy credential metadata (permitted scopes, expiry, delegation chain depth) without revealing the operator. Wallet-to-wallet interactions reference DIDs rather than ephemeral keypairs, enabling reputation accumulation across protocols.

new_components_needed.description: Wallet provider adapter (@bolyra/wallet-adapter) — thin SDK wrapper that speaks the Privy/Coinbase/TON wallet initialization protocols, maps their key management to Bolyra's EdDSA operator key, and exposes prove/verify as wallet-native RPC methods. Needs provider-specific key derivation bridges (secp256k1 wallet key → Baby Jubjub operator key via deterministic derivation).

WalletReputationAccumulator — on-chain contract that records successful handshake count per agent commitment hash (not per address) without linking interactions. Enables wallets to build verifiable reputation ('this agent has completed 47 verified handshakes') without identity correlation. Reads from IdentityRegistry events.

Credential serialization for wallet transport — AgentPolicy proofs need a compact wire format (EIP-712 typed data or CBOR envelope) that wallets can attach to transactions as calldata or pass in WalletConnect sessions. Current SDK returns raw snarkjs proof objects; wallets need a standardized envelope.

EIP/BIP-style proposal for 'ZKP Credential Attachment in Wallet Transactions' — defines how a Bolyra proof envelope rides alongside a standard EVM transaction, enabling any wallet to verify the agent's identity without protocol-specific integration. Extends draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 with wallet-transaction binding semantics.

No new circuit needed, but AgentPolicy requires a minor modification: add an optional walletCommitment public input (Poseidon hash of the wallet's secp256k1 public key) that binds the credential to a specific wallet address, preventing credential portability across wallets when the operator wants address-binding. This is a ~200-constraint addition.

positioning_alignment: This opportunity is a textbook privacy-layer-under-incumbents play. Privy, Coinbase, and TON are building the wallet UX and managing the keys — Bolyra slots underneath as the identity attestation layer they don't want to build themselves. The value proposition is precise: 'your agentic wallet already has a keypair; Bolyra wraps it in a verifiable credential that proves who authorized it, what it can do, and that the human behind it is unique — without revealing any of that to the counterparty.' This avoids competing on wallet UX (Privy wins there) or on-chain infrastructure (Coinbase wins there) and instead occupies the credential layer that neither wants to own. The selective-disclosure property of AgentPolicy is the key differentiator vs. simple keypair identity: wallets can prove minimum viable trust ('I can spend up to $100') without revealing maximum capability ('I can also sign on behalf of the operator'). This is the exact gap the agentic wallet ecosystem hasn't filled, and Bolyra's existing circuit stack fills it with zero new trusted setup.

deliverables.name: wallet-adapter-privy
deliverables.description: Thin adapter (~200 LOC) that wraps @bolyra/sdk's createAgentCredential and proveHandshake behind Privy's embedded-wallet signer interface. Maps Privy's session key to Bolyra's operator EdDSA key, auto-populates model hash from the agent config, and returns PLONK proofs the wallet can attach to transactions.

deliverables.name: wallet-scope-mask-helpers
deliverables.description: Add permissionsToBitmask presets for common wallet operations (SWAP, BRIDGE, STAKE, TRANSFER) that map to the existing 8-bit cumulative encoding. Add a verifyScopeSufficient(proof, requiredOps) convenience method that checks the bitmask without a full proof re-verification.

deliverables.name: wallet-handshake-demo
deliverables.description: End-to-end Node script: two agentic wallets (Alice-trading-bot, Bob-gas-optimizer) each create AgentPolicy credentials, perform mutual proveHandshake, and verify each other's scope. Outputs proof artifacts and verification result to stdout. No UI, no server — just a runnable script proving the flow works.

deliverables.name: wallet-identity-quickstart
deliverables.description: Developer guide: 'Give your agentic wallet a verifiable identity in 10 minutes'. Covers credential creation, scope selection, handshake with a counterparty, and delegation to sub-agents. Published as docs/quickstart-agentic-wallets.md.

deliverables.name: registry-wallet-scope-query
deliverables.description: Add a public view function walletScopeValid(bytes32 nullifier, uint8 requiredMask) to IdentityRegistry that lets on-chain protocols check whether a wallet's last verified proof covers a required permission scope without re-verifying the full proof. ~30 LOC Solidity + tests.

risks: Privy's embedded-wallet signer API is undocumented/unstable — adapter may break on their next release. Mitigate by coding against their public types and pinning the SDK version.

Demand signal is speculative (web search returned no evidence). Without real wallet teams requesting this, the adapter may sit unused. Mitigate by shipping the demo as a standalone proof-of-concept and measuring GitHub stars/forks.

PLONK proof size (~2KB) may exceed gas-optimized wallet transaction payload budgets on L2s. Mitigate by benchmarking proof size in the demo and documenting the tradeoff vs Groth16 (smaller but needs ceremony).
Solo-founder risk: 9 days of focused work competes with protocol-autoresearch and other loops. Mitigate by time-boxing to 2 calendar weeks with hard cut.
mvp.success_criteria: The wallet-handshake-demo script runs end-to-end: two wallets create credentials, mutually verify via proveHandshake, and the on-chain walletScopeValid check passes on a local Hardhat fork — all within a single `npm run demo:wallet` command. Secondary: at least one external developer (from Privy, Coinbase, or TON ecosystem) runs the quickstart and provides feedback within 30 days of publish.

title: Privacy-Preserving Cross-App Agent Access (XAA Alternative)
description: Okta's XAA protocol centralizes app-to-app agent connections under one identity fabric, creating a single point of trust and surveillance. Bolyra's mutual ZKP handshake offers a decentralized alternative where agents prove authorization across app boundaries without a central broker seeing every connection. Target enterprises with multi-vendor agent deployments.

rationale.demand: Web search evidence returned zero results and no URLs were provided; the premise that enterprises want a decentralized alternative to Okta XAA is plausible but entirely unsubstantiated with named buyers or engagement metrics.
rationale.timing: Okta XAA is a real announced initiative but no competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or regulatory deadlines are cited — the window is speculative with no dated timeline anchors.
rationale.fit: All three core circuits (HumanUniqueness, AgentPolicy, Delegation) map directly to the cross-app delegation chain with only minor SDK-layer extensions needed; ZKP provides a genuine privacy advantage over centralized broker visibility.
rationale.feasibility: MVP spec is well-scoped at ~10 days reusing existing primitives with no new circuits, though cross-domain Merkle root discovery and multi-app trust bootstrapping introduce real integration complexity that the spec underestimates.

mapped_primitives.usage: Each agent proves it holds valid operator-signed credentials with the required permission bitmask for the target app's scope — no central broker needs to see the credential. The selective disclosure capability (requiredScopeMask) is directly applicable: App B can demand proof that the agent has WRITE_DATA + FINANCIAL_SMALL without learning the full permission set.

Cross-app agent access is fundamentally a delegation chain: Human → Agent A (in App 1) → Agent B (in App 2). The existing 3-hop delegation circuit with scope narrowing enforces that App 2's agent can only act within the permissions App 1's agent was granted, never escalate. This is the core differentiator vs XAA's centralized policy enforcement.

The human root of trust anchoring the delegation chain. Proves a real human authorized the original agent without revealing which human — critical for cross-app scenarios where App 2 should not learn the user's identity in App 1. Nullifier scoping ensures sybil resistance per-app without cross-app linkability.

On-chain verification of cross-app handshakes. The atomic handshake verification (Groth16 + PLONK in single tx) can validate that both the originating agent and the target agent have valid credentials. Root history buffers handle the case where enrollment trees update between proof generation and verification across apps.

proveHandshake and verifyHandshake are the SDK entry points for the cross-app flow. The existing API shape works — the gap is orchestrating multi-party handshakes where the verifier is a different app than the one that issued the agent credential.

new_components_needed.description: Cross-App Handshake Profile: an extension to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 specifying the message flow when prover and verifier are in different trust domains. Must define how App B discovers App A's Merkle root (direct on-chain lookup vs relayed root attestation), session nonce negotiation across app boundaries, and error semantics when the originating app's tree is unknown.

CrossAppScopeAttestation: optional circuit that proves an agent's credential was issued by a recognized operator (Merkle membership in an operator registry) without revealing which operator. Needed because in cross-app scenarios, App B needs assurance the agent comes from a legitimate App A operator without learning the operator's identity. Could be folded into AgentPolicy as an optional input path.

OperatorRegistry: lightweight on-chain registry of recognized operator public keys (or commitments). Apps register their operator keys; the CrossAppScopeAttestation circuit proves membership. Simpler than XAA's full identity fabric — just a Merkle tree of operator commitments with add/revoke.

Multi-party handshake orchestration: extend the SDK to support a three-party flow (human-origin app agent, target app agent, target app verifier). Primarily session management and nonce coordination — the cryptographic primitives already exist.

Gateway/proxy adapter for common agent frameworks (LangChain, CrewAI) that intercepts cross-app tool calls and transparently attaches Bolyra proofs. This is the adoption surface — enterprises won't rewrite agent code, they need a drop-in middleware.

positioning_alignment: This is a textbook privacy-layer-under-incumbents play. Okta's XAA creates value by connecting apps but extracts rent by forcing all connections through their identity fabric — every cross-app agent call is visible to Okta. Bolyra slots underneath as the proof layer that makes the connections private: enterprises get the interoperability XAA promises without giving Okta (or any single vendor) a panopticon view of their agent topology. The positioning is 'use whatever identity provider you want — Okta, Entra, Ping — but prove cross-app authorization with ZKPs instead of bearer tokens that the broker can inspect.' This avoids competing with Okta on IdP features (where they have distribution) and instead competes on a property Okta structurally cannot offer: the broker not being able to see what it's brokering.

deliverables.name: Cross-App Handshake Profile
deliverables.description: Extension to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 defining message flow when prover and verifier are in different trust domains. Covers: cross-domain Merkle root discovery (on-chain lookup), session nonce negotiation across app boundaries, error semantics for unknown trees. No new circuit — reuses existing Delegation chain with an additional scope-binding constraint in the SDK layer.

deliverables.name: SDK cross-app handshake methods
deliverables.description: Add proveXAppHandshake(human, agent, targetAppDomain) and verifyXAppHandshake(proof, originAppRoot, nonce) to @bolyra/sdk. Wraps existing proveHandshake with cross-domain nonce binding (H(sessionNonce || targetAppDomain)) and origin-app Merkle root relay. No new circuits — reuses AgentPolicy + Delegation proofs with an additional domain-binding signal.

deliverables.name: Cross-app scope narrowing validation
deliverables.description: Extend validateCumulativeBitEncoding to enforce that cross-app delegated permissions are a strict subset of the originating app's grant. Add requiredScopeMask parameter to verifyXAppHandshake so target app declares minimum permissions. Pure SDK logic, no circuit changes.

deliverables.name: Two-app demo
deliverables.description: Minimal Node script simulating App A (document signing) delegating to App B (payment processor). Human enrolls, grants agent WRITE_DATA+FINANCIAL_SMALL in App A, agent proves to App B it can do FINANCIAL_SMALL without revealing WRITE_DATA or human identity. Uses existing circuits with the new SDK methods. README with architecture diagram.

deliverables.name: Contract test: cross-app root verification
deliverables.description: Hardhat test proving that IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake accepts proofs generated against a different app's enrollment root when that root is in the on-chain root history buffer. Validates the existing rootHistoryExpiry mechanism works for cross-app scenarios. No new contract code expected — just test coverage.

deliverables.name: Conformance test vectors
deliverables.description: Add cross-app test vectors to spec/conformance/ covering: valid cross-app handshake, scope escalation rejection, expired origin root rejection, nonce replay rejection. JSON format matching existing test vector structure.

risks: Cross-app Merkle root discovery adds latency — if App B must query on-chain for App A's root, handshake round-trip could exceed enterprise SLA expectations
No real enterprise demand signal yet (web search failed, timing assessed at 18+ months) — this could be building ahead of market
CrossAppScopeAttestation circuit (operator anonymity) is deferred — enterprises may require it before adoption, adding future circuit work
Okta XAA may ship a 'good enough' centralized solution before decentralized alternatives gain traction, collapsing the window
mvp.success_criteria: End-to-end demo where two independent Node processes (App A, App B) complete a cross-app ZKP handshake in under 5 seconds, with the delegation circuit enforcing that App B's agent cannot exceed App A's permission grant, and conformance tests passing for all 4 rejection scenarios (scope escalation, expired root, nonce replay, unknown origin).

title: ZKP-Based Role Verification for AutoGen Multi-Agent Systems
description: AutoGen's discussion #4656 shows teams struggling with agent authorization in multi-agent orchestrations. Bolyra's cumulative-bit permissions and Delegation circuit map directly to role-based access control where agents prove capability tiers without exposing the full permission set. This is a pure protocol fit.

rationale.demand: Web search evidence returned zero results and the only cited source is a single GitHub discussion (#4656) with no verified engagement metrics, named buyers, or independent corroboration.
rationale.timing: No competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or regulatory deadlines were provided; the timing assessment itself says 18+ months out with no concrete anchors.
rationale.fit: All three core circuits (AgentPolicy, Delegation, HumanUniqueness) map directly to the RBAC pattern with no new circuit work required, and the SDK already exposes the needed entry points — but the opportunity is integration-layer work, not a novel primitive unlock.
rationale.feasibility: The MVP spec is realistic at ~9 days using existing primitives and the Python subprocess bridge, though AutoGen's API stability and the thin demand evidence add execution risk.

mapped_primitives.usage: Agents in an AutoGen group chat prove they hold a required permission tier (e.g. FINANCIAL_MEDIUM) via selective disclosure of the requiredScopeMask bits, without revealing the full 8-bit bitmask. Each orchestrator step can demand a different scope mask, enforcing least-privilege per task.

AutoGen's GroupChat manager delegates scoped authority to worker agents. The Delegation circuit enforces one-way scope narrowing (delegateeScope ⊂ delegatorScope) and expiry narrowing, mapping directly to AutoGen's pattern of a coordinator spawning specialized sub-agents with reduced privileges. The 3-hop limit covers coordinator → specialist → sub-task chains.

Human-in-the-loop approval steps in AutoGen workflows (UserProxyAgent) bind a unique human to the orchestration via nullifier-based sybil resistance, proving a real operator authorized the chain without revealing identity across sessions.

On-chain root history buffers let AutoGen orchestrations verify that an agent's credential was valid at orchestration-start time even if the tree updates mid-run. Atomic handshake verification covers the human-approves-agent-action pattern. Delegation nullifier replay protection prevents a revoked sub-agent from re-entering a group chat.

createAgentCredential and proveHandshake are the runtime entry points. AutoGen agents call proveHandshake at orchestration join time; the orchestrator calls verifyHandshake before routing messages. permissionsToBitmask maps AutoGen role names to Bolyra's cumulative-bit encoding.

new_components_needed.description: autogen-bolyra adapter: a ConversableAgent mixin or middleware that intercepts AutoGen's message routing to require/verify Bolyra proofs before an agent can participate in a GroupChat. Wraps proveHandshake on agent registration and verifyHandshake on message dispatch. Needs to map AutoGen's role strings (e.g. 'coder', 'critic', 'executor') to Bolyra permission bitmasks.

Role-to-bitmask registry: a declarative mapping layer (JSON config or builder API) that lets AutoGen users define role → permission-bit mappings without manually constructing bitmasks. e.g. { 'executor': ['READ_DATA', 'WRITE_DATA', 'FINANCIAL_SMALL'], 'auditor': ['READ_DATA'] }. Thin wrapper over existing permissionsToBitmask.

RBAC profile appendix to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 specifying how multi-agent orchestration frameworks map role hierarchies to cumulative-bit permission encoding, including recommended scope masks for common patterns (read-only observer, financial approver, code executor).

AutoGen GroupChatManager hook for delegation chain verification: when a coordinator delegates to a sub-agent that further delegates, the manager verifies the full chain on-chain via IdentityRegistry.verifyDelegation before allowing the deepest agent to act. Requires wiring AutoGen's speaker-selection callback to a Bolyra verification call.

positioning_alignment: Pure privacy-layer-under-incumbent play. AutoGen (Microsoft) owns the orchestration framework and will never build ZKP infrastructure themselves — it's orthogonal to their core value prop of multi-agent conversation patterns. Bolyra slots in as the authorization layer that AutoGen teams adopt when they move from demo to production and realize they need agent-level access control without a centralized permission server. The integration is additive (existing AutoGen code unchanged, Bolyra wraps the message router), which matches the low-friction adoption model. Competitive moat: no other ZKP identity project has cumulative-bit selective disclosure + delegation chain verification in a single SDK call, which is exactly what multi-agent RBAC requires.

deliverables.name: role-bitmask-registry
deliverables.description: Declarative JSON config + builder API mapping role strings (e.g. 'executor', 'auditor') to Bolyra cumulative-bit permission masks. Thin wrapper over existing permissionsToBitmask with validation that enforces cumulative-bit implication rules.

deliverables.name: autogen-bolyra-adapter
deliverables.description: Python package (bolyra-autogen) providing a BolyraGroupChat subclass that intercepts agent registration and message dispatch. On join: agent calls proveHandshake via the Python SDK subprocess bridge. On message: orchestrator calls verifyHandshake against the role's required scope mask. Wraps AutoGen's ConversableAgent with a BolyraAgent mixin that holds credential state.

deliverables.name: role-config-schema
deliverables.description: JSON Schema for the role-to-permission mapping file. Defines the contract between AutoGen orchestration configs and Bolyra's permission model. Includes 3 preset profiles: minimal (read-only agents), standard (read/write), financial (tiered spending).

deliverables.name: autogen-rbac-demo
deliverables.description: Working example: 3-agent AutoGen GroupChat (coordinator, coder, executor) where the executor proves FINANCIAL_SMALL before running paid API calls, the coder proves WRITE_DATA, and a human-in-the-loop step uses HumanUniqueness. Includes a README walkthrough.
deliverables.reuses: AgentPolicy circuit, Delegation circuit, HumanUniqueness circuit

deliverables.name: integration-tests
deliverables.description: Pytest suite covering: role mapping validation, proof generation/verification round-trip via subprocess bridge, delegation chain narrowing (coordinator delegates to sub-agent with fewer bits), and rejection of over-privileged agents. Uses mock proofs (fast mode).

deliverables.name: autogen-rbac-docs
deliverables.description: Technical guide: how Bolyra's permission model maps to AutoGen roles, architecture diagram of proof flow in a GroupChat, API reference for BolyraGroupChat and BolyraAgent, and a quickstart for the demo.

risks: AutoGen's API surface is unstable (v0.4 rewrote agent interfaces) — adapter may break on minor releases
Python SDK subprocess bridge adds ~2s latency per proof; may be too slow for real-time multi-agent chat loops
Discussion #4656 demand signal is thin — a GitHub discussion is not validated purchasing intent
Web search evidence returned nothing — no corroborating signal that teams are actually blocked on this
AutoGen community may prefer lightweight API-key RBAC over ZKP complexity for most use cases
mvp.success_criteria: Demo runs end-to-end: 3-agent GroupChat where an under-privileged agent is rejected, a properly-credentialed agent is admitted, and a delegation chain narrows scope across hops. Published to integrations/autogen/ with passing CI. Secondary signal: 5+ GitHub stars or 2+ forks within 30 days of announcement on AutoGen discussions.
